food_name: Beijiang Chicken with Bone (Da Pan Ji)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Da Pan Ji / Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken. Mapped to broader 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for braised bone-in chicken noodle dishes is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for this braised bone-in chicken dish with noodles and vegetables. No composite dish code for Da Pan Ji exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific composite code for Xinjiang-style braised chicken with noodles. A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary protein anchor. The composite nature of the dish (noodles, potatoes, bell peppers) is not fully captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Da Pan Ji or Xinjiang braised chicken dish. SNOMED CT food coverage for regional Chinese composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Da Pan Ji is a large communal plate of approximately 1000 g, shared among 2–4 people, containing bone-in chicken pieces, hand-pulled noodles, potatoes, and bell peppers in a spiced braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed (FDC ID 171477) combined with USDA SR Legacy noodle and potato component estimates; adapted for composite Xinjiang-style braised chicken dish
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Da Pan Ji as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for stewed bone-in chicken (FDC ID 171477), cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168917), boiled potato (FDC ID 170026), and bell pepper (FDC ID 170108), weighted to reflect the approximate ingredient proportions of this dish (chicken ~40%, noodles ~30%, potato ~15%, vegetables and sauce ~15%). The input record's per-100g values (energy 180 kcal, protein 12 g, fat 9 g, carbohydrate 12 g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 350 mg/100g reflects heavy soy sauce use typical of this preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283 — analogous estimate based on wheat noodles (GI ~58–65) and potato (GI ~70–78) weighted composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Da Pan Ji (Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on a weighted composite of the two dominant carbohydrate sources: hand-pulled wheat noodles (GI ~58–65, per University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles/pasta) and boiled potato (GI ~70–78, per University of Sydney GI Database). Weighting reflects approximately 65% noodle contribution and 35% potato contribution to total carbohydrate. Protein (12 g/100g) and fat (9 g/100g) from chicken and oil moderate the overall glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 120) / 100 = 78.0, where carbohydrate per 1000 g serving = 120 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350 mg/100g from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and added salt; a full 1000 g serving delivers approximately 3500 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and salt during braising, limit portion size to 200–300 g, and increase the proportion of bell peppers and onions to partially offset sodium with potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Composite GI of approximately 65 (medium-high) with a glycaemic load of 78 per full serving; hand-pulled wheat noodles and potato are both rapidly digested refined carbohydrates contributing 12 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1 g dietary fibre.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request reduced noodle quantity or substitute with lower-GI alternatives such as shirataki or wholegrain noodles, and retain the chicken protein component which partially moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.5 g/100g and cholesterol 45 mg/100g from bone-in chicken skin and cooking oil; sodium 350 mg/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request skin-removed chicken, reduced cooking oil, and lower soy sauce quantities, and limit portion to 200–300 g to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardioprotective targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bell peppers, dried chillies, and potatoes are nightshade vegetables containing solanine, which may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; refined carbohydrates from noodles and potato also contribute to pro-inflammatory glycaemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should request substitution of bell peppers and potatoes with non-nightshade vegetables, retain ginger and cumin for their anti-inflammatory properties, and reduce noodle portion to lower glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Da Pan Ji (大盘鸡), literally 'big plate chicken', is a celebrated Xinjiang-style Chinese dish originating from the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region of northwestern China. The dish features bone-in chicken pieces braised in a deeply aromatic, mildly spicy sauce built from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, star anise, cumin, ginger, and garlic — a spice profile that reflects the Silk Road culinary heritage of the region. The chicken is first stir-fried with aromatics to develop colour and flavour, then slow-braised with potato chunks and bell peppers until tender. The dish is traditionally served over or alongside wide, hand-pulled wheat noodles (la mian) that absorb the rich braising sauce. The result is a hearty, communal meal with a complex flavour profile: savoury, mildly spicy, slightly sweet from the dark soy sauce, and deeply umami. Texture contrasts include tender fall-off-the-bone chicken, soft potato, and chewy hand-pulled noodles. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 180 kcal, 12 g protein, 9 g fat (2.5 g saturated), and 12 g carbohydrate with 1 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is high at 350 mg/100g due to soy sauce and salt. The estimated composite glycaemic index is 65 (medium), with a glycaemic load of 78 per full 1000 g sharing plate, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is clinically relevant for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GL per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium). It is commonly served as a large sharing plate for 2–4 people in Chinese restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No single approved source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Da Pan Ji as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component entries for stewed bone-in chicken, cooked wheat noodles, boiled potato, and bell pepper, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. The input record's per-100g values are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat noodles and potato. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct dish-level measurement.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite braised chicken dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish are not directly available in an approved source; estimated ~270 mg/100g from component analysis but not reported as a confirmed value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Xinjiang-style braised chicken dish are not available as a unified dish entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.05 g/100g from the input record; this trace value is plausible from cooking oil used in braising and is retained. 5. Halal flag is set to true as chicken is the primary protein and no pork or alcohol ingredients are inherent to the traditional recipe; however, individual restaurant preparations should be verified. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for Da Pan Ji exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not correspond to the official HPB nutrient database; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central component estimates as the approved source.
